tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
 1) The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
 2) Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)
<<image_vec_align>>

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 23.33 cycles. At this rate:
 - 6% of peak load performance is reached (13.03 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 23.33 to 21.47 cycles (1.09x speedup).

header:
Warnings:

 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R10,%RDX,1),%RSI] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x20(%R12),%RBX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RCX,%RAX,8),%RAX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%RAX,%R12,1),%R8] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x2(%RBX),%R8D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x1(%R9),%R10D] is unknown
12% of peak computational performance is used (2.91 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 23.33 to 19.67 cycles (1.19x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<<image_col_maj>>
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
61% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 66% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 71% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 50% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
 - 49% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 59% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 23.33 to 18.80 cycles (1.24x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.05x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 3 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

The loop is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:950-956.
Analyzed code is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:950,953-956.
Warnings:
 - Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
 - Ignoring paths for analysis
 - Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 128 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=128
 - RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
 - Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.
This loop has 128 execution paths.
The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX

Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)
